answer:
The correct answer is **\boxed{A}**.

The argument is invalid because when \( M \) and \( N \) are both false, \( \sim(M \equiv \sim N) \) becomes \( \sim(\text{false} \equiv \text{true}) = \sim(\text{false}) = \text{true} \), while the premise \( M \) (false) does not logically follow from the conclusion. This satisfies the counterexample in option A.
